text: The surgeon begins the procedure by making an incision to open the peritoneum, exposing the underlying structures for the transverse colectomy.
The surgeon dissects over the middle colic vessels, moving towards the bowel wall while ensuring a ten-centimeter margin, indicating a focus on maintaining good blood supply in this area.
The surgeon navigates over the ligament of Treitz towards the planned colon margin, maintaining alignment with the previously marked trajectory.

The surgeon marks the distal colon margin approximately ten centimeters from the planned resection site for visual reference. The surgeon then proceeds to open the tissue over the middle colic pedicle, elevating the area for better access.
The surgeon creates a window to the right of the middle colic vessels and proceeds towards the bowel wall within the previously created leaflet.
The surgeon exposes the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) during a right colon procedure, allowing for clear identification and tracing of the vessels encountered.
The surgeon observes pulsations in the wad of tissue and opens over the area where the superior mesenteric vein (SMV) enters the middle colic pedicle.
The surgeon visually identifies the middle colic artery and vein, which are positioned together within the surgical field.
The surgeon creates a window around the middle colic vessels, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue to expose the area.
The surgeon releases the tension on the tissue and proceeds to transect the targeted structure.
The surgeon accesses the lesser sac and proceeds to detach tissue from the base of the pancreas, utilizing the created window for clear visualization.
The surgeon visually identifies the stomach in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the root of the mesentery up to the scored area, extending the dissection towards the distal colon margin.

The surgeon divides the omentum at the planned proximal and distal margins, splitting it vertically. The surgeon then enters the lesser sac through the gastrocolic omentum to detach residual attachments to the transverse colon.
The surgeon addresses adhesions between the hepatic flexure and the gallbladder, ensuring there is sufficient redundant distal colon for anastomosis despite tension from the flexures.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence to assess the perfusion of the distal colon margin, ensuring it is well-perfused before proceeding with the division.

The surgeon cauterizes the bowel margin on the right side, initially marking it for resection. Indocyanine green (ICG) imaging reveals that the well-perfused margin is actually located more proximally.
The surgeon prepares to retrieve the resected colon specimen from the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon inspects the dissection site, observing how the two ends of the colon align closely for anastomosis.
The surgeon aligns the two bowel ends using threo silk sutures and proceeds to perform a sewn end-to-end anastomosis, joining the bowel segments together.
The surgeon begins the anastomosis by sewing the two staple lines together, a standard technique used in colectomies.
The surgeon ties the threostase suture to secure the sewn end-to-end anastomosis, completing the connection between the two bowel segments.
The surgeon has created colotomies, which are incisions in the colon, as part of the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon checks the fluorescence of the tissue using indocyanine green (ICG) imaging to assess perfusion.
The surgeon sutures the common channel closed, using a technique consistent with previous colectomy procedures, as the two staple lines are sewn together.
The surgeon extracts the specimen through a small Pfannenstiel incision, which is located in the lower abdomen.
The surgeon brings two sutures towards the midline to tie them together, completing the closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon sews the last anterior outer layer using two PDS sutures, completing the closure of the surgical site.
